summary:
The Mitanni kingdom, located in northern Mesopotamia and northern Syria roughly from 1500 to 1300 BC, was one of the major powers of the Late Bronze Age despite being the least well-documented of the great kingdoms of the period. Its ruling and military elite spoke an Indo-Aryan language related to Sanskrit, while the bulk of the population spoke Hurrian, a language isolate unrelated to the Semitic or Indo-European language families. This linguistic duality has led scholars to propose that the Mitanni state was founded by Indo-Aryan warriors who migrated southwestward and established themselves as a dominant ruling class over Hurrian-speaking peoples.

At its height, the Mitanni kingdom controlled territory from the Zagros Mountains in the east to the Euphrates bend in the west, and its kings competed with Egypt and the Hittites for dominance over northern Syria. Mitanni texts invoke the Vedic deities Mitra, Varuna, Indra, and the Nasatyas in treaty oaths — remarkable evidence connecting Mitanni Indo-Aryan culture to the religious traditions preserved in the Indian [[event:1847|Rigveda]]. The Mitanni also pioneered [[event:1859|chariot]] horse training techniques, and a training manual for chariot horses written by a Mitanni horse trainer named Kikkuli survives in Hittite translation.

The Mitanni kingdom was progressively weakened by the expansion of both the Hittites and the [[event:1830|Middle Assyrian Empire]] during the fourteenth century BC. The Hittite king Suppiluliuma I dismantled Mitanni power around 1344–1322 BC, and Assyria completed its absorption of Mitanni territory by around 1300 BC. The Hurrian cultural legacy survived in the art, religion, and administrative practices of the successor states, including significant influence on Hittite culture.
